author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
Why the Righteousness Which is of the Law is Valued Slightly by Paul.
title: Why the Righteousness Which is of the Law is Valued Slightly by Paul.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore the blessed Paul casts away those past attainments of his righteousness, as “losses” and “dung,” that “he may win Christ and be found in Him, not having his own righteousness, which is of the law.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Wherefore his own, if it is of the law?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For that law is the law of God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Who has denied this, save Marcion and Manicheus, and such like pests?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Since, then, that is the law of God, he says it is “his own” righteousness “which is of the law;” and this righteousness of his own he would not have, but cast it forth as “dung.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Why so, except because it is this which I have above demonstrated, that those are under the law who, being ignorant of the righteousness of God, and going about to establish their own, are not subject to the righteousness of God?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For they think that, by the strength of their own will, they will fulfil the commands of the law; and wrapped up in their pride, they are not converted to assisting grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Thus the letter killeth them either openly, as being guilty to themselves, by not doing what the law commands; or by thinking that they do it, although they do it not with spiritual love, which is of God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Thus they remain either plainly wicked or deceitfully righteous,—manifestly cut off in open unrighteousness, or foolishly elated in fallacious righteousness.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And by this means—marvellous indeed, but yet true—the righteousness of the law is not fulfilled by the righteousness which is in the law, or by the law, but by that which is in the Spirit of grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Because the righteousness of the law is fulfilled in those, as it is written, who walk not according to the flesh, but according to the Spirit.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But, according to the righteousness which is in the law, the apostle says that he was blameless in the flesh, not in the Spirit; and he says that the righteousness which is of the law was his, not God’s.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: It must be understood, therefore, that the righteousness of the law is not fulfilled according to the righteousness which is in the law or of the law, that is, according to the righteousness of man, but according to the righteousness which is in the Spirit of grace, therefore according to the righteousness of God, that is, which man has from God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Which may be thus more clearly and briefly stated: That the righteousness of the law is not fulfilled when the law commands, and man as it were of his own strength obeys; but when the Spirit aids, and man’s free will, but freed by the grace of God, performs.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore the righteousness of the law is to command what is pleasing to God, to forbid what is displeasing; but the righteousness in the law is to obey the letter, and beyond it to seek for no assistance of God for holy living.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For when he had said, “Not having my own righteousness, which is of the law, but that which is by the faith of Christ,” he added, “Which is from God.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: That, therefore, is itself the righteousness of God, being ignorant of which the proud go about to establish their own; for it is not called the righteousness of God because by it God is righteous, but because man has it from God.
